reasoning: The 1-year Treasury bill rate recently stood at 3.90% (June 20), slightly below its 30-day moving average of ~3.95%. The FRED statistical prediction model estimates a 67% chance that the rate will exceed its current baseline by June 29 due to mean reversion to the short-term trend line. I anchor my initial near-term probability at 0.65. Looking further out, macroeconomic indicators point to competing forces. On one side, the Federal Reserve's June 2025 meeting concluded with projections for two 25-bps rate cuts by year-end, which should theoretically drag the forward-looking 1-year yield down as expectations for the overnight rate drop. On the other side, persistent PCE inflation (3.6% in Q1 2025) and anticipated tariff policies pose significant 'higher for longer' upside risks to yields. Forecasting these macro policies 6 months out carries very high variance. Per the instructional constraint that 'uncertainty should INCREASE with forecast horizon' and probabilities must approach 0.5, I smoothly taper the probabilities from 0.65 on the short-term date (June 29) down to 0.52 for the most distant horizon (Dec 19).
